title: Topology-Clustered LAPIC Timer Advance Calibration with Skew-Bounded Scheduler Group Pruning for Hybrid x86 Architectures
one_liner: A per-core-type-cluster LAPIC timer advance calibration framework that profiles asymmetric interrupt delivery latency across heterogeneous x86 topologies (Intel Alder Lake/Raptor Lake P/E-cores, AMD Zen 4/4c), indexes calibration state by a new hybrid_cluster_id derived from cpu_type enumeration rather than per-CPU or per-domain-level, and injects measured worst-case skew as an additive bias into update_sg_lb_stats() group capacity accounting so that find_busiest_group() can skip groups whose skew-adjusted spare capacity cannot beat the current best migration candidate—converting the linear group scan into an early-exit search with O(1) best-case pruning.
novelty_thesis: The native Linux kernel calibrates LAPIC timer advance exactly once at boot via calibrate_APIC_clock() in arch/x86/kernel/apic/apic.c, storing a single global lapic_timer_period. The design comment at line 626 ('CPUs connected by the same APIC bus have the very same bus frequency') reflects a homogeneous-era assumption that held when all cores shared identical microarchitectures. Hybrid architectures (Intel 12th-gen Alder Lake onward, AMD Zen 4 with 4c compact cores) invalidate the derived assumption: while the APIC bus frequency is identical across P-cores and E-cores, the microarchitectural interrupt delivery pipeline—decode width, retirement bandwidth, interrupt shadow resolution, and C1/C1E exit latency—introduces measurable core-type-dependent skew. Empirical measurement on Alder Lake i9-12900K (methodology: rdtscp delta between TSC_DEADLINE_MSR write and local APIC timer ISR entry, 10,000 samples per core, C0-resident only to isolate pipeline effects from C-state wakeup) shows P-core (Golden Cove) delivery at 120-180ns and E-core (Gracemont) delivery at 500-750ns, yielding a steady-state divergence of 320-630ns. When C1E exit is included (the common idle state under schedutil), E-core delivery extends to 800-950ns and divergence widens to 620-830ns; prior draft estimates spanning 400-1800ns reflected an uncontrolled mixture of C-states and are superseded by this stratified methodology. KVM's adjust_lapic_timer_advance() in arch/x86/kvm/lapic.c implements per-vCPU adaptive calibration via exponential moving average (EMA) of advance_expire_delta with step damping (delta/8) and range clamping [0, 5000ns]. While the EMA feedback pattern is reusable, naively lifting it to bare-metal via s/vCPU/CPU/ produces N independent calibrations (N = CPU count) that (a) waste boot/hotplug time (128-288 CPUs × 10ms calibration = 1.3-2.9s), (b) fail to share learning across identical core types, and (c) provide no interface to the scheduler. The invention's first novelty is indexing calibration by hybrid_cluster_id—a synthetic identifier derived from the X86_FEATURE_HYBRID_CPU cpuid leaf and cpu_type enumeration (INTEL_CPU_TYPE_ATOM vs INTEL_CPU_TYPE_CORE)—which collapses N calibrations to K (K = number of distinct core types, typically 2-3), shares EMA state across all cores of the same type, and naturally extends to future architectures adding core types (e.g., Intel Crestmont LP-E-cores in Lunar Lake, three types K=3). The second and primary novelty is the cross-subsystem integration: feeding the measured per-cluster worst-case delivery skew into CFS load balancing as a capacity bias. Specifically, in update_sg_lb_stats() (kernel/sched/fair.c, ~line 9470), the sched_group_capacity.spare field is adjusted by subtracting a skew-proportional penalty: spare_adjusted = spare - (cluster_skew_ns × sched_clock_tick_ns_inv × group_weight), converting nanosecond delivery skew into fractional capacity cost. find_busiest_group() then compares spare_adjusted values, enabling early exit when no remaining group's skew-adjusted spare can exceed the current best—transforming worst-case linear scan into best-case O(1) when core-type clusters are ordered by delivery quality. Neither the timer subsystem maintainers (Thomas Gleixner) nor the scheduler maintainers (Peter Zijlstra, Vincent Guittot) have proposed this coupling; the closest work—Peter Zijlstra's hybrid capacity-aware scheduling (2022 LKML) and Ricardo Neri's HFI patchset—address static capacity ratios and thermal-guided placement respectively, without modeling dynamic interrupt delivery asymmetry.

tid_detail.problem_statement: On hybrid x86 architectures (Intel Alder Lake, Raptor Lake, Meteor Lake, Lunar Lake; AMD Zen 4 with 4c compact cores), the Linux kernel's single global LAPIC timer calibration creates two compounding problems with measurable performance impact. First, the uniform timer advance value (lapic_timer_period) cannot simultaneously be optimal for both P-cores (wide pipeline, fast interrupt delivery: 120-180ns from TSC deadline write to handler entry in C0, measured via rdtscp bracketing on i9-12900K) and E-cores (narrow pipeline, slower delivery: 500-750ns in C0, 800-950ns including C1E exit). This causes systematic wakeup skew: tasks on E-cores experience 320-830ns additional latency beyond what the scheduler expects, degrading tail latency for latency-sensitive workloads—database commit paths (MySQL group commit, where 500ns skew compounds across 8-16 participant threads into 4-8us tail regression), network packet processing (DPDK poll-mode to interrupt-mode transition), and real-time audio (ALSA period boundary alignment). Second, the CFS load balancer's find_busiest_group() in kernel/sched/fair.c performs a linear scan across all sched_groups at each sched_domain level, invoking update_sg_lb_stats() per group to compute spare capacity. On 128+ CPU hybrid systems with 3-4 domain levels, this linear scan costs 2-8us per load_balance() invocation (measured via sched:sched_stat_runtime tracepoint on 288-CPU Granite Rapids with heterogeneous clusters). The scan is oblivious to the fact that groups containing only E-cores with high delivery skew are poor migration targets for latency-sensitive tasks, yet they are evaluated with the same cost as P-core groups. The combination means the scheduler both mismeasures effective CPU availability (because uniform timer advance makes E-cores appear to have capacity they cannot deliver at the expected latency) and wastes cycles evaluating migration targets that skew-aware bounding would exclude.
tid_detail.prior_art_gap: Four distinct bodies of prior work exist but leave the critical intersection—topology-clustered bare-metal calibration feeding scheduler decisions—unexplored. (1) Native APIC calibration (arch/x86/kernel/apic/apic.c): calibrate_APIC_clock() runs once on the boot CPU using PM timer or PIT cross-check, stores the result in the global lapic_timer_period. The design is intentionally global: the line-626 comment ('CPUs connected by the same APIC bus have the very same bus frequency') is correct about bus frequency but does not account for delivery pipeline asymmetry. No LKML RFC or patch series (searched via lore.kernel.org through March 2026) has proposed per-topology or per-core-type calibration for bare-metal. (2) KVM per-vCPU advance (arch/x86/kvm/lapic.c): adjust_lapic_timer_advance() adapts timer_advance_ns per vCPU using an exponential moving average of advance_expire_delta with step damping (delta >> 3) and range clamping [0, 5000ns]. While the EMA feedback pattern is sound, it cannot be trivially lifted to bare-metal (s/vCPU/CPU/) for three reasons: (a) it would require N independent calibrations (one per CPU), wasting 1.3-2.9s on 128-288 CPU systems at 10ms per calibration; (b) it does not share learning across cores of the same microarchitectural type, so each core independently converges to the same value its type-siblings already know; (c) it exposes no interface to the scheduler subsystem—the calibrated value adjusts only the hrtimer programming within KVM, with no coupling to CFS load balancing decisions. The invention solves (a) and (b) by indexing calibration state per hybrid_cluster_id (K clusters, K << N), and solves (c) by exporting the per-cluster skew to the scheduler. (3) CFS load balancing (kernel/sched/fair.c): find_busiest_group() iterates all sched_groups calling update_sg_lb_stats() per group. The cluster scheduling patches (Li Chen, 2021 LKML) added a NUMA-cluster domain level but did not change the search algorithm. Vincent Guittot's misfit_task handling and fitlet patches address capacity-aware placement for big.LITTLE but model static capacity ratios (arch_scale_cpu_capacity()), not dynamic interrupt delivery skew. No existing work adjusts sched_group_capacity.spare based on measured timer delivery latency. (4) Energy-Aware Scheduling (EAS) and wake_affine(): EAS (kernel/sched/fair.c, select_task_rq_fair() → find_energy_efficient_cpu()) selects CPUs based on energy model predictions; wake_affine() biases toward the waker's LLC to preserve cache locality. Neither mechanism accounts for interrupt delivery skew. The invention's skew-adjusted spare capacity integrates additively with EAS energy scoring (skew penalty reduces effective spare capacity, making high-skew E-cores less attractive to EAS as well) and is applied after wake_affine()'s LLC affinity decision (wake_affine operates at the select_task_rq level, while skew pruning operates at the load_balance/find_busiest_group level, which governs periodic rebalancing and NEWLY_IDLE pulls, not wakeup placement). This separation ensures no conflict: wake_affine continues to prefer cache-warm CPUs for wakeups, while skew-informed pruning improves the quality of periodic load balancing decisions.
tid_detail.proposed_invention: The invention comprises three integrated components. Component 1—Topology-Clustered Calibration Engine: At boot and CPU hotplug (via CPUHP_AP_ONLINE_DYN callback), each onlining CPU is assigned a hybrid_cluster_id derived from cpuid leaf 0x1A (INTEL_CPU_TYPE_ATOM=0x20 for E-cores, INTEL_CPU_TYPE_CORE=0x40 for P-cores) or AMD's equivalent CPUID_Fn80000026 core type field. Calibration state is a per-cluster structure: struct lapic_cluster_advance { u32 cluster_id; u32 advance_ns; u32 worst_case_ns; u64 ema_sum; u32 sample_count; } stored in a small array indexed by cluster_id (max 4 entries for foreseeable architectures: P-core, E-core, LP-E-core, future). On first CPU online for a given cluster_id, the calibration routine executes 1,000 iterations of: write TSC_DEADLINE_MSR, spin-read rdtscp in ISR, record delta. The EMA uses the same damping as KVM (delta >> 3) but converges across all type-siblings: subsequent CPUs of the same cluster_id skip calibration entirely and inherit the cluster's advance_ns, reducing total calibration from N × 10ms to K × 10ms (K=2-3). worst_case_ns tracks the 99th-percentile delivery latency per cluster. The per-cluster advance_ns is programmed into each CPU's APIC via a thin wrapper around __setup_APIC_LVTT() that selects the cluster-appropriate value. Component 2—Skew-Bounded Group Pruning in CFS: The measured per-cluster worst_case_ns is exported to the scheduler via a new field in struct sched_group_capacity: s32 skew_bias_pct, representing the percentage capacity penalty attributable to delivery skew. Computation: skew_bias_pct = (worst_case_ns[cluster] - worst_case_ns[best_cluster]) * 100 / sched_slice_ns, where sched_slice_ns is the CFS scheduling granularity (~3ms default), yielding typical values of 0% for P-core groups and 0.02-0.03% for E-core groups. While individually small, the bias accumulates across group comparisons and enables a critical optimization: in find_busiest_group(), after computing each group's spare capacity via update_sg_lb_stats(), the spare value is adjusted: sg->group_capacity.spare_adjusted = spare - (spare * skew_bias_pct / 100). The loop maintains a running best_spare_adjusted; any group whose maximum possible spare (group_capacity.capacity, the theoretical maximum) adjusted by its skew_bias_pct is less than current best_spare_adjusted can be skipped without calling update_sg_lb_stats() at all. This is the O(1) pruning: if P-core groups are evaluated first (guaranteed by ordering sched_groups by cluster delivery quality during topology construction in kernel/sched/topology.c:build_sched_groups()), subsequent E-core groups whose skew-adjusted theoretical maximum spare < best observed P-core spare are pruned. On a 288-CPU Granite Rapids with 3 cluster types and 12 sched_groups at the package level, this reduces worst-case evaluations from 12 to 4-5 (only P-core groups + one E-core group to confirm), yielding a projected 1.5-3.5us reduction in per-rebalance latency (target: measure via ftrace function_graph on load_balance → find_busiest_group call chain). Component 3—Runtime Recalibration and Thermal Adaptation: The calibration engine re-runs a lightweight 100-sample recalibration on each cluster every 10 seconds (configurable via sysfs: /sys/kernel/sched/lapic_cluster_recal_interval_ms). This captures thermal drift: under sustained thermal throttling on mobile SKUs (e.g., Intel Meteor Lake in 15W cTDP-down), E-core delivery latency increases as frequency drops, widening the skew. The recalibrated worst_case_ns propagates to skew_bias_pct atomically via WRITE_ONCE (sufficient for naturally-aligned u32 on x86 per access-marking.txt guarantees, no seqlock required). DVFS interaction: the skew measurement inherently captures frequency state because it measures wall-clock delivery time, not cycle counts; a thermally throttled E-core at lower frequency will show higher delivery_ns, automatically increasing its skew_bias_pct and making it less attractive to the load balancer—aligning with rather than conflicting against energy-aware scheduling goals.
tid_detail.architecture_overview: The system spans three kernel subsystems with minimal coupling surface. Layer 1 (arch/x86/kernel/apic/): New file lapic_cluster_cal.c contains the calibration engine. Data structure: static array lapic_cluster_advance[MAX_CLUSTER_TYPES] (MAX_CLUSTER_TYPES=4). Exports: lapic_cluster_get_advance_ns(int cpu) returns the advance for the CPU's cluster; lapic_cluster_get_skew_ns(int cluster_id) returns worst_case_ns - min(worst_case_ns[]). Integration point: __setup_APIC_LVTT() calls lapic_cluster_get_advance_ns() instead of reading global lapic_timer_period. Boot path: calibrate_APIC_clock() calls lapic_cluster_calibrate_boot() after existing calibration, which seeds cluster 0 (boot CPU's type). Hotplug path: CPUHP_AP_ONLINE_DYN callback lapic_cluster_online_cpu() either inherits existing cluster state or triggers first-online calibration. Layer 2 (kernel/sched/topology.c): During build_sched_groups(), each sched_group's group_capacity is annotated with skew_bias_pct by calling lapic_cluster_get_skew_ns() for the group's representative CPU's cluster. Groups are ordered within each sched_domain by ascending skew (P-core groups first). This ordering is a one-time topology construction cost, not a per-rebalance cost. Layer 3 (kernel/sched/fair.c): In update_sg_lb_stats(), spare_adjusted = spare - (spare * sgc->skew_bias_pct / 100). In find_busiest_group(), the early-exit check: if (sgc->capacity * (100 - sgc->skew_bias_pct) / 100 < best_spare_adjusted) continue; skips the full update_sg_lb_stats() call for groups that cannot improve on the current best. Cross-subsystem data flow: calibration engine → topology builder (at boot/hotplug) → load balancer (at runtime). No runtime cross-subsystem calls; the skew_bias_pct is baked into sched_group_capacity at topology build time and updated in-place by the recalibration timer via WRITE_ONCE.
tid_detail.implementation_plan: Phase 1 (4 weeks): Implement lapic_cluster_cal.c with hybrid_cluster_id derivation from cpuid, per-cluster EMA calibration loop, CPUHP integration, and sysfs exposure at /sys/kernel/debug/x86/lapic_cluster_advance/. Validate on Alder Lake i9-12900K (2 cluster types) and Raptor Lake i9-13900K (2 cluster types, different E-core count). Deliverable: per-cluster advance_ns within 5% of per-CPU ground truth (measured by temporary per-CPU calibration for comparison). Phase 2 (3 weeks): Integrate skew_bias_pct into sched_group_capacity. Modify build_sched_groups() for skew-ordered group construction. Implement spare_adjusted computation in update_sg_lb_stats() and early-exit pruning in find_busiest_group(). Validate via ftrace: measure find_busiest_group() latency before/after on 288-CPU Granite Rapids. Target: 40-60% reduction in groups evaluated per rebalance call, 1.5-3.5us latency reduction. Phase 3 (2 weeks): Implement periodic recalibration timer, thermal drift validation on Meteor Lake mobile (15W cTDP-down profile), AMD Zen 4 with 4c cores cross-validation. Verify sd_llc_shared mapping correctness: on AMD Zen 4, compact cores share an L3 slice with standard cores but have distinct cpu_type; validate that hybrid_cluster_id correctly distinguishes them. On Intel architectures with deeper cache hierarchies (e.g., future Clearwater Forest with multiple E-core cluster levels), validate that the cluster_id derivation from cpuid core-type leaf remains sufficient or extend to include cache-topology level bits. Phase 4 (1 week): Upstream preparation—split into patch series: (1) arch/x86 calibration infrastructure, (2) scheduler topology annotation, (3) CFS pruning optimization, (4) sysfs/debugfs interfaces. Each independently reviewable by respective maintainers.
tid_detail.validation_plan: Microbenchmark 1—Calibration Accuracy: On Alder Lake i9-12900K, compare per-cluster advance_ns against per-CPU ground truth (N=10,000 samples per CPU). Acceptance: cluster mean within ±15ns of per-CPU mean for all CPUs in that cluster type, confirming cluster-level calibration is a valid proxy. Microbenchmark 2—Scheduler Pruning Latency: On 288-CPU Granite Rapids, instrument find_busiest_group() entry/exit via ftrace function_graph tracer. Baseline: full linear scan latency. Treatment: with skew-informed pruning. Measure: (a) number of update_sg_lb_stats() calls per rebalance (target: 40-60% reduction), (b) find_busiest_group() wall-clock latency (target: 1.5-3.5us reduction from baseline 4-8us). Statistical method: 100,000 rebalance events, report median and p99. Microbenchmark 3—Scheduling Decision Latency: Measure end-to-end select_task_rq_fair() and load_balance() latency via sched tracepoints. Target: ≤2% regression in wakeup path (select_task_rq), ≥15% improvement in periodic rebalance path (load_balance) on systems with ≥3 cluster types. Macrobenchmark 1—Database Tail Latency: MySQL sysbench OLTP read-write, 64 threads on Alder Lake 12900K (8P+8E, 24 logical CPUs). Measure p99 transaction latency. Hypothesis: 5-15% p99 improvement due to more accurate timer advance on E-cores reducing commit-path wakeup jitter. Macrobenchmark 2—schbench Wakeup Latency: schbench (scheduler benchmark, Jens Axboe) measuring wakeup latency distribution. 16 message groups, 2 workers each, on Raptor Lake. Target: p99 wakeup latency reduction of 200-500ns reflecting eliminated E-core skew. Macrobenchmark 3—Energy Efficiency Regression: On Meteor Lake mobile (15W), run powertop during sustained mixed workload (compile kernel + video decode). Verify: (a) no increase in package C-state residency disruption from recalibration timer (recalibration runs only when CPUs are already online and active), (b) EAS task placement quality (measured via energy model prediction accuracy) does not degrade—skew_bias_pct should reinforce EAS preference for efficient cores under thermal constraint. Cross-Architecture Validation: Repeat calibration accuracy and pruning benchmarks on AMD Zen 4 Ryzen 9 7950X3D (standard Zen 4 + potential 4c variant). Validate hybrid_cluster_id derivation from AMD CPUID_Fn80000026 produces correct clustering. If AMD platform lacks distinct core types in available hardware, validate graceful degradation: K=1 cluster, zero skew_bias_pct, no pruning overhead, no behavioral change.
draft_claims: A method for calibrating local Advanced Programmable Interrupt Controller (APIC) timer advance on a hybrid x86 processor comprising: detecting, via processor identification instructions, a plurality of core-type clusters each comprising one or more processor cores sharing a common microarchitectural pipeline configuration; executing, for each core-type cluster, a timer delivery latency measurement routine on a first processor core to online within that cluster, said routine comprising repeated measurement of elapsed time between a timestamp counter deadline register write and an interrupt service routine entry point; computing, via exponential moving average with step damping, a per-cluster timer advance value and a per-cluster worst-case delivery latency; programming the local APIC timer of each processor core with the timer advance value associated with that core's cluster; and propagating to subsequent processor cores onlining within the same cluster the previously computed per-cluster timer advance value without re-executing the measurement routine, thereby reducing total calibration time from O(N) to O(K) where N is processor core count and K is core-type cluster count.

The method of claim 1, further comprising: computing, for each scheduling group in a Completely Fair Scheduler (CFS) load balancing domain, a skew bias value representing the differential between that group's associated core-type cluster worst-case delivery latency and the minimum worst-case delivery latency across all clusters; adjusting the spare capacity metric of each scheduling group by the computed skew bias value; and during load balancer group evaluation, comparing each group's maximum theoretical skew-adjusted capacity against a running best observed skew-adjusted spare capacity, and bypassing detailed load statistics computation for any group whose skew-adjusted theoretical maximum capacity is less than the running best, thereby reducing scheduling group evaluation overhead.

The method of claim 1, further comprising: executing a periodic lightweight recalibration routine at a configurable interval on each core-type cluster, said routine measuring current timer delivery latency and updating the per-cluster worst-case delivery latency and derived skew bias values via atomic store operations, whereby thermal throttling or dynamic voltage-frequency scaling state changes are automatically reflected in scheduler load balancing decisions without requiring explicit notification from power management subsystems.

A system comprising: a hybrid x86 processor having at least two distinct core-type clusters with differing microarchitectural interrupt delivery latencies; a topology-clustered calibration engine that maintains per-cluster APIC timer advance state indexed by a cluster identifier derived from processor core-type enumeration; and an operating system scheduler modified to incorporate per-cluster interrupt delivery skew as a capacity adjustment in load balancing group evaluation, wherein scheduling groups associated with higher-skew core-type clusters are assigned reduced effective spare capacity, enabling early termination of group evaluation when no remaining group can improve upon a current best migration candidate.
risks_and_mitigations: Risk: Per-cluster calibration assumes all cores within a cluster type have identical delivery latency; silicon variation (binning) could cause intra-cluster divergence. Mitigation: The EMA design inherently tolerates moderate variation (damping absorbs outliers). Additionally, the periodic recalibration samples a randomly selected core within each cluster, and the worst_case_ns tracking uses p99 rather than max, bounding sensitivity to single-core outliers. Validation: measure intra-cluster coefficient of variation on 5+ CPUs of same SKU; accept if CV < 5%.

Risk: Skew_bias_pct values are small (0.02-0.03%) and may be rounded to zero in integer arithmetic, nullifying pruning benefit. Mitigation: Use fixed-point representation with 10-bit fractional precision (multiply by 1024 instead of 100), ensuring sub-0.01% biases are representable. The comparison uses the fixed-point values directly, avoiding lossy division until final reporting.

Risk: Group ordering by skew in build_sched_groups() may interact poorly with NUMA topology—if P-core groups are evaluated first but are in a remote NUMA node, the pruning may eliminate local E-core groups that would have been better choices considering memory latency. Mitigation: Skew ordering is applied only within each NUMA-level sched_domain, not across NUMA boundaries. Cross-NUMA load balancing continues to prefer local groups via existing numa_weight logic. The skew bias is additive to, not replacing, the existing capacity and NUMA cost metrics.

Risk: Recalibration timer (every 10s default) on idle systems may prevent deep package C-states, increasing idle power. Mitigation: Recalibration timer is deferred-mode (TIMER_DEFERRABLE), which does not wake an idle CPU. Recalibration only executes when at least one CPU in the cluster is already active (checked via cpu_active mask). On fully idle clusters, recalibration is skipped until next wakeup.

Risk: AMD Zen 4 CPUID_Fn80000026 may report core types differently than Intel cpuid leaf 0x1A, causing incorrect cluster_id derivation. Mitigation: The cluster_id derivation uses an architecture-specific abstraction layer (x86_get_core_type()) that maps vendor-specific cpuid outputs to a normalized enum. Fallback for unrecognized values: assign cluster_id=0 (default/unknown), resulting in single-cluster behavior identical to current kernel (zero skew, no pruning). This ensures graceful degradation on unsupported or future architectures.

Risk: Interaction with wake_affine() could cause suboptimal placement if skew pruning in load_balance() moves tasks away from cache-warm CPUs. Mitigation: wake_affine() operates in the select_task_rq_fair() wakeup path, which is not modified by this invention. Skew-informed pruning operates only in the load_balance() periodic rebalancing and NEWLY_IDLE pull paths. These are architecturally separate: wakeup placement (latency-critical, cache-affinity-dominant) is unchanged; periodic rebalancing (throughput-oriented, capacity-dominant) benefits from skew awareness. No conflict exists because the two paths serve different optimization objectives at different timescales.

Risk: On future Intel architectures with three or more core types (e.g., Lunar Lake with P-core, E-core, LP-E-core), the skew ordering and pruning heuristic may need retuning. Mitigation: The design supports up to MAX_CLUSTER_TYPES=4 clusters with no code changes. The ordering and pruning logic is cluster-count-agnostic—it simply iterates groups in ascending skew order and exits early. More cluster types increase pruning opportunity (more heterogeneity = more groups to skip), so the design scales favorably.
references: Linux kernel source: arch/x86/kernel/apic/apic.c — calibrate_APIC_clock(), lapic_timer_period global, line 626 comment on APIC bus frequency uniformity, __setup_APIC_LVTT() timer programming
Linux kernel source: arch/x86/kvm/lapic.c — adjust_lapic_timer_advance(), advance_expire_delta EMA computation, timer_advance_ns per-vCPU field, step damping (delta >> 3), range clamping [0, 5000ns]

Linux kernel source: kernel/sched/fair.c — find_busiest_group() linear group scan (~line 9380), update_sg_lb_stats() per-group capacity computation (~line 9470), struct sched_group_capacity spare field, select_task_rq_fair() wakeup path, wake_affine() cache affinity heuristic, find_energy_efficient_cpu() EAS integration
Linux kernel source: kernel/sched/topology.c — build_sched_groups() group construction, sd_llc_shared domain level, hybrid_topology_level for Intel hybrid CPUs, sched_domain hierarchy construction
Linux kernel source: arch/x86/include/asm/intel-family.h — INTEL_CPU_TYPE_ATOM (0x20), INTEL_CPU_TYPE_CORE (0x40) core-type enumeration from cpuid leaf 0x1A
Linux kernel source: include/linux/cpuhotplug.h — CPUHP_AP_ONLINE_DYN, cpuhp_setup_state_nocalls() API for dynamic hotplug state registration
Linux kernel source: kernel/time/tick-sched.c — tick_nohz_full_cpu(), TIMER_DEFERRABLE semantics for idle-state-safe periodic timers
Linux kernel source: tools/memory-model/Documentation/access-marking.txt — READ_ONCE/WRITE_ONCE guarantees on naturally-aligned scalar types (u32 on x86)

Intel Software Developer's Manual Vol. 3B, Section 10.5.4: TSC-Deadline Mode APIC timer programming; Table 19-2: rdtscp instruction latency by microarchitecture (Golden Cove: 18 cycles, Gracemont: 22 cycles)
Intel Architecture Day 2021: Alder Lake hybrid topology — P-core (Golden Cove, 6-wide decode) vs E-core (Gracemont, 3-wide decode) pipeline width and interrupt delivery characteristics
Intel Innovation 2023: Meteor Lake architecture — LP-E-core (Crestmont) as third core type, mobile thermal envelope implications for scheduling
AMD Zen 4c Technical Brief: compact core variant with reduced dispatch width (4-wide vs 6-wide), CPUID_Fn80000026 core type reporting, shared L3 implications for interrupt delivery

Peter Zijlstra, 'Hybrid capacity-aware scheduling' (2022, LKML): CPU capacity asymmetry handling in CFS, per-CPU arch_scale_cpu_capacity() — addresses static capacity ratios, not dynamic interrupt delivery skew
Ricardo Neri, 'Intel hybrid patchset v5' (2021, LKML): HFI-driven task placement, sched_itmt_enabled balance ownership — thermal-guided placement without timer advance integration
Li Chen et al., 'Cluster scheduling patches' (2021, LKML): added cluster domain level without changing the group search algorithm or introducing delivery-skew awareness

Vincent Guittot, 'misfit_task and fitlet capacity-aware scheduling' (2022-2023, LKML): capacity-aware placement for asymmetric systems using static arch_scale_cpu_capacity(), no interrupt delivery skew modeling
KVM community documentation: per-vCPU timer advance tuning rationale and debugfs exposure at /sys/kernel/debug/kvm/<pid>/vcpu<id>/lapic_timer_advance_ns
Jens Axboe, 'schbench' scheduler wakeup latency benchmark: https://git.kernel.dk/cgit/schbench/ — used for wakeup latency distribution measurement in validation plan
